coredata.dc:title: Glutathione-decorated fluorescent carbon quantum dots for sensitive and selective detection of levodopa
coredata.dc:creator: Park, Seok Won
Kim, Tae Eun
Jung, Yun Kyung
coredata.dc:description: Levodopa has been a standard drug for treating Parkinson’s disease since the 1960s, but it has caused many side effects such as wearing-off, motor fluctuation, and dystonia. In this work, we developed glutathione-conjugated carbon quantum dots (GSH-CQDs) as a novel fluorescent sensor for sensitive and selective detection of levodopa. The GSH-CQDs were prepared by EDC/NHS coupling reaction of glutathione (GSH) with amine-functionalized CQDs (N-CQDs) synthesized using meta-phenylenediamine and ethylenediamine. The synthesized GSH-CQDs emitted bright green fluorescence with a high quantum yield (QY) of 22.42 ± 6.88%. However, upon the addition of levodopa to GSH-CQDs under alkaline conditions, the fluorescence of GSH-CQDs was quenched. Since levodopa is converted to dopaquinone in an alkaline environment, it is presumed that thiol groups of GHS-CQDs form covalent bonds with dopaquinone, causing fluorescence quenching through photoinduced electron transfer. Therefore, as the concentration of levodopa increased, the fluorescence intensity of GSH-CQDs was gradually decreased. Under optimal conditions, a linear response was observed in the range of 0.05–1 μM, and limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 0.057 μM. The GSH-CQDs exhibited high specificity to levodopa over other non-target biological substances, quinone derivatives, and Parkinson’s medications. Furthermore, the capability of this GSH-CQDs sensor for monitoring levodopa in human serum were validated with excellent precision and recovery rates of 100.20–103.33%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot (CQD)
Glutathione (GSH)
Levodopa
Dopaquinone
Fluorescent sensor
Photoinduced electron transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of amine-functionalized carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
Preparation of glutathione-conjugated carbon quantum dots (GSH-CQDs)
Sensitive and selective detection of levodopa by GSH-CQDs fluorescent sensor
Optimization of detection conditions for levodopa
Sensitive and selective detection of levodopa

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Korea Ministry of Trade Industry and Energy
Korea Ministry of Science ICT and Future Planning

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthesis of GSH-CQDs. (b) Levodopa detection by fluorescence quenching of green fluorescent GSH-CQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Characterization of GSH-CQDs. (a) TEM image of GSH-CQDs (Inset: Size distribution and HRTEM image of GSH-CQDs). (b) XRD pattern of GSH-CQDs. (c) FT-IR spectra of N-CQDs, GSH, and GSH-CQDs. (d) XPS spectrum of GSH-CQDs. (e) UV/vis absorbance, fluorescence excitation (λem = 515 nm), and fluorescence emission (λex = 410 nm) spectra of GSH-CQDs (Inset: Photographs of GSH-CQDs under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right)). (f) Fluorescence emission spectra of GSH-CQDs depending on excitation wavelengths.
Fluorescence quenching efficiency of the GSH-CQDs toward the detection of levodopa depending on (a) pH, (b) reaction temperature, (c) concentration of GSH-CQDs, and (d) reaction time.

Sensitive detection of levodopa with the GSH-CQDs fluorescent sensor. (a) Photographs of the GSH-CQDs after the interaction with increasing concentrations of levodopa under daylight and 365 nm UV light. (b) Fluorescence response of the GSH-CQDs upon increasing concentrations of levodopa from 0 to 50 μM. (c) The linear response between fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0–F/F0) of the GSH-CQDs and indicated concentrations of levodopa (0–1.0 μM).

Specificity of levodopa detection by the GSH-CQDs fluorescent sensor. (a) Fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0–F/F0) of the GSH-CQDs after addition of (1) 1 μM of levodopa and 250 μM of non-target biological substances including (2) alanine, (3) ascorbic acid, (4) citric acid, (5) cysteine, (6) glutamine, (7) glycine, and (8) urea. (b) Photographs corresponding to the samples shown in (a) under daylight and 365 nm UV light.
(a) Measurement of levodopa in human serum via the spike and recovery method. (b) Comparison of analytical performance between our sensor and previously reported methods for levodopa detection.

row.entry: Prussian Blue modified carbon screen-printed electrode

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: To determine the levodopa concentration, a calibration curve was used to determine the amount of levodopa in human serum.Mean of three measurements.

head.ce:title: Glutathione-decorated fluorescent carbon quantum dots for sensitive and selective detection of levodopa

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanoscience and Engineering, Inje University, Gimhae, 50834, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nanoscience and Engineering, Inje University, Gimhae, 50834, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biomedical Engineering, Inje University, Gimhae, 50834, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biomedical Engineering, Inje University, Gimhae, 50834, Republic of Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Nanoscience and Engineering & School of Biomedical Engineering, Inje University, Gimhae, 50834, Republic of Korea.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Nanoscience and Engineering & School of Biomedical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Levodopa has been a standard drug for treating Parkinson’s disease since the 1960s, but it has caused many side effects such as wearing-off, motor fluctuation, and dystonia. In this work, we developed glutathione-conjugated carbon quantum dots (GSH-CQDs) as a novel fluorescent sensor for sensitive and selective detection of levodopa. The GSH-CQDs were prepared by EDC/NHS coupling reaction of glutathione (GSH) with amine-functionalized CQDs (N-CQDs) synthesized using meta-phenylenediamine and ethylenediamine. The synthesized GSH-CQDs emitted bright green fluorescence with a high quantum yield (QY) of 22.42 ± 6.88%. However, upon the addition of levodopa to GSH-CQDs under alkaline conditions, the fluorescence of GSH-CQDs was quenched. Since levodopa is converted to dopaquinone in an alkaline environment, it is presumed that thiol groups of GHS-CQDs form covalent bonds with dopaquinone, causing fluorescence quenching through photoinduced electron transfer. Therefore, as the concentration of levodopa increased, the fluorescence intensity of GSH-CQDs was gradually decreased. Under optimal conditions, a linear response was observed in the range of 0.05–1 μM, and limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 0.057 μM. The GSH-CQDs exhibited high specificity to levodopa over other non-target biological substances, quinone derivatives, and Parkinson’s medications. Furthermore, the capability of this GSH-CQDs sensor for monitoring levodopa in human serum were validated with excellent precision and recovery rates of 100.20–103.33%.

ce:section.ce:para: Parkinson’s disease is a neurodegenerative disease that occurs primarily in the elderly and affects more than 10 million people worldwide [1,2]. Until today, the etiology of Parkinson’s disease has been unknown, but it is believed to be due to a deficiency of dopamine in the brain [3]. Most of the clinical symptoms were rigidity, tremors, and bradykinesia, which could profoundly affect the lives of people suffering from Parkinson’s disease [4]. L-3,4-dihydroxyphenylalanine (levodopa), a precursor of neurotransmitter dopamine, has been universally prescribed as a gold standard for the treatment of Parkinson’s disease since the 1960s [5]. Unlike Dopamine, levodopa can cross the blood-brain barrier and be converted into dopamine via L-aromatic amino acid decarboxylase [6]. As a result, it increases dopamine levels in the brain, which alleviates the symptoms caused by Parkinson’s disease. However, chronic administration of levodopa causes side effects such as hypotension, arrhythmias, nausea, confusion, wearing-off, and random on-off [7,8], which makes it difficult to treat Parkinson’s disease. Therefore, the sensitive and selective detection of levodopa is very necessary to evaluate a patient’s condition and prevent side effects.

To date, many researchers have reported several analytical methods for levodopa detection, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9,10], electrochemical measurement [11,12], and optical methods (quantum dots and noble nanoparticles) [13,14]. However, these methods suffer from several shortcomings, including low sensitivity, long measurement periods, and the requirements of expensive instruments and skilled technicians. Especially, the electrochemical method has the disadvantage that it is difficult to distinguish between levodopa, ascorbic acid, and uric acid owing to similar oxidation peaks [15]. And, since the optical methods generally use quantum dots composed of heavy metals or noble nanoparticles synthesized by using expensive noble metals such as gold, silver and titanium, there are issues of toxicity and price, respectively. From this perspective, the fluorescence-based detection method has been studied in recent years due to their relatively high sensitivity, low cost, easy to use, and visual confirmation. In this regard, zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a new series of fluorescent nanomaterials can be considered as attractive optical alternatives because of its low cost, easiness to synthesize, eco-friendliness, unique fluorescence, excellent biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and excellent photostability [16]. Accordingly, they have been in the limelight in various fields such as bioimaging [17], drug delivery systems [18], optronics devices [19], energy devices [20], catalysts [21], and biosensors [22]. The CQDs capable of convenient functionalization are useful platforms to construct a novel fluorescent sensor for levodopa detection.

In this study, we designed a novel glutathione-conjugated carbon quantum dots (GSH-CQDs) for sensitive and selective detection of levodopa. As illustrated in Fig. 1 , amine-functionalized carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized via microwave-assisted pyrolysis method using meta-phenylenediamine and ethylenediamine. And then, the surface of N-CQDs was modified with glutathione (GSH) through EDC/NHS reaction. The as-prepared green fluorescent GSH-CQDs could successfully detect levodopa by fluorescence quenching of GSH-CQDs, which is clearly visible to the naked eye under 365 nm UV light. Under alkaline conditions, levodopa converts to dopaquinone, which binds to the thiol group of GSH. This causes photoinduced electron transfer between fluorescent GSH-CQDs and dopaquinone, leading to fluorescence quenching of GSH-CQDs. The detection limit of levodopa in the GSH-CQDs sensor was determined and the selectivity of the GSH-CQDs toward levodopa was investigated over other non-target biological substances, quinone derivatives, and Parkinson’s medications. Also, recovery rates of spiked levodopa in human serum sample were obtained, which shows great potential in clinical applications.

Meta-phenylenediamine, ethylenediamine, 3,4-dihydroxyphenylalanine (levodopa), human serum, fluorescein, sodium hydroxide, alanine, ascorbic acid, citric acid, cysteine, glutamine, urea, anthraquinone, p-benzoquinone, 1,4-naphthoquinone, benserazide hydrochloride, carbidopa, ropinirole hydrochloride, and 5,5′-dithiobis(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Hydrochloric acid was obtained from Samchun (Korea). Glycine was bought from Bioshop (Canada). Silica gel was obtained from Millipore (USA). Hydrogen peroxide and sodium chloride were bought from Duksan (Korea). Deionized water was used throughout the experiments. All reagents were used as received, without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of amine-functionalized carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The amine-functionalized carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized using the microwave-assisted pyrolysis method. In 40 mL of deionized water, 1 mmol of meta-phenylenediamine and 6 mmol of ethylenediamine were dissolved. The solution was heated in a domestic microwave oven (700 W) for 2 min. The solution was cooled to room temperature naturally and then purified through column chromatography using silica gel (70–230 mesh). Then, the N-CQDs were stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of glutathione-conjugated carbon quantum dots (GSH-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Carboxyl group of Glutathione (GSH) was conjugated onto the surface of N-CQDs through EDC/NHS coupling process. The GSH (1 mM) was mixed with deionized water (8 mL, pH = 7) containing 2.5 mM of EDC and 5 mM of NHS. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 15 min in dark. Afterward, 2 mL of N-CQDs solution was added to the mixture, followed by stirring under dark ambient conditions for 4 h. Subsequently, the mixture was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 20 min to remove undissolved substances. Then, the GSH-CQDs (4.66 ± 0.95 mg mL−1) were stored at 4 °C for further use.

The QY of GSH-CQDs was determined by comparing the integrated fluorescence intensity and absorbance with those of a reference fluorescein (QY = 0.925) dissolved in ethyl alcohol (refractive index: 1.36). To minimize reabsorption, absorbance values of GSH-CQDs and fluorescein were maintained at approximately 0.05. The QY of the GSH-CQDs were calculated according to the following equation:
Q
Y
C D
=
Q
Y
R E F
×
A
R E F
A
C D
×
I
C D
I
R E F
×
η
C
D 2
η
R E
F 2
where A is the absorbance of the samples, I is the integrated fluorescence intensity of the samples, and η is the refractive index of solvent in which samples were dissolved, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of levodopa by GSH-CQDs fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Before starting the detection of levodopa, optimization experiments were performed. The optimal response conditions of the GSH-CQDs toward levodopa were determined by changing pH, temperature, the concentration of GSH-CQDs, and reaction time. Under optimum conditions, various concentrations (0–50 μM) of levodopa were added to GSH-CQDs solution (250 μg mL−1) and reacted for 30 min. Before and after the addition of levodopa to GSH-CQDs, the fluorescence emission spectra of the GSH-CQDs were collected in the wavelength from 420 nm to 800 nm. In order to validate the selectivity of GSH-CQDs to levodopa, 250 μM of other biological substances (alanine, ascorbic acid, citric acid, cysteine, glutamine, glycine, and urea), 10 μM of quinone derivatives (anthraquinone, p-benzoquinone, and 1,4-naphthoquinone), and 10 μM of Parkinson’s medications (benserazide, carbidopa, and ropinirole) were incubated with GSH-CQDs and emission spectra of the GSH-CQDs were obtained. The detection conditions were the same as mentioned above.

To evaluate the clinical applicability of GSH-CQDs, a spike recovery assay was performed in human serum. 250 μg mL−1 of the GSH-CQDs were reacted with 50-fold diluted human serum (2%) containing different concentrations (1, 3, and 5 μM) of levodopa for 30 min. The recovery rates of spiked levodopa in human serum sample were calculated by measuring the changes in the fluorescence intensity of the GSH-CQDs.

The UV/vis absorbance spectrum of GSH-CQDs were recorded using a Cary 60 UV/vis spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA). The fluorescence properties were measured by an RF-6000 spectrofluorophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was analyzed using an Ultima Ⅳ X-ray diffraction system (Rigaku, Japan). The Raman spectra were obtained via a NRS-5100 (JASCO, Japan). Fourier transform-infrared spectra (FTIR) were measured by a IRSpirit Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the GSH-CQDs were obtained via a JEM-2100F microscope (JEOL, Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of the GSH-CQDs were obtained by means of ESCALAB 250XI electron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The samples were drop-cast onto the silicon wafer for XPS measurements. The PL lifetimes of GSH-CQDs and GSH-CQDs + Dopaquinone were scanned using Fluorolog3 spectrofluorometer (HORIBA Scientific, Japan).

To develop a novel fluorescent sensor for detection of levodopa, N-CQDs and GSH-CQDs were synthesized sequentially. Structural morphologies and crystallinity of both GSH-CQDs and N-CQDs were characterized by TEM, XRD, and Raman spectroscopy. Fig. 2 a shows that the GSH-CQDs were quasi-spherical, with an average diameter of 4.37 ± 0.87 nm. The N-CQDs had spherical shape with an average diameter of 3.80 ± 1.26 nm (Figure S1a). Additionally, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (inset of Fig. 2a & inset of Figure S1a) exhibits that both have a crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to (100) plane of graphitic carbon [23]. XRD patterns of the GSH-CQDs and N-CQDs showed two broad peaks centered at 2θ = 28° and 2θ = 42°, which were attributed to the (002) and (100) lattice spacing of graphitic carbon (Fig. 2b & Figure S1b), suggesting that both had a highly crystalline structure [24,25]. As shown in Figure S2, Raman spectra of both GSH-CQDs and N-CQDs display the characteristic D band at 1355 cm−1 and the G band at 1580 cm−1, which corresponds to sp3-hybridized carbon and sp2-hybridized crystalline carbon, respectively. The ratios of D band to G band (ID/IG) of GSH-CQDs and N-CQDs were calculated to be 0.89, and 0.92, respectively. Since ID/IG ratio less than 1 indicates a highly graphitic carbon structure [26], it implies that our GSH-CQDs and N-CQDs were well graphitized. These results confirmed that the decoration of N-CQDs with GSH increases the size of CQDs but does not affect their crystallinity.

The FT-IR spectra were analyzed to identify surface functional groups of N-CQDs, GSH, and GSH-CQDs (Fig. 2c). The N-CQDs showed peaks at 3160 cm−1 (N–H stretching), 2920 cm−1 and 2810 cm−1 (C–H stretching), and 1592 cm−1 (N–H bending) [27–29]. The GSH spectra displayed peaks at 2920 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1 (C–H stretching), 1743 cm−1 (CO stretching of carboxylic acid), 1657 cm−1 (CO stretching of amide Ⅰ), and 1540 cm−1 (C–S stretching). The successful conjugation of N-CQDs with GSH was confirmed by the presence of peaks at 3170 cm−1 (significantly reduced N–H stretching), 2920 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1 (C–H stretching), 1657 cm−1 (CO stretching of amide Ⅰ), 1540 cm−1 (C–S stretching), and 1470 cm−1 (enhanced C–N stretching of amide Ⅱ). In company with the FT-IR, XPS measurements further confirmed the successful GSH conjugation on the N-CQDs. Fig. 2d clearly shows peaks corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s, and S2p, with atomic percentages of 46.21%, 18.15%, 27.55%, and 2.06%, respectively. Since the sample was drop-casted on silicon wafer, there are silicon peaks in the XPS spectra. The deconvoluted high-resolution spectra of C1s, N1s, O1s, and S2p provided the detailed functional groups, indicating CC (284.9 eV), CO (287.3 eV), pyridinic N (398.9 eV), pyrrolic N (400.5 eV), CO (530.3 eV), C–OH/C–O–C (531.3 eV), C–S–H (163.1 eV), and C–S (167.4 eV) groups within the GSH-CQDs (Figure S3) [30–32]. Whereas the XPS spectra of N-CQDs (Figure S1c-f) didn’t show any sulfur peak coming from GSH. These results indicate that GSH-CQDs were successfully constructed.

To further confirm successful synthesis of GSH-CQDs based on optical properties, absorbances of precursor, N-CQDs, GSH, and GSH-CQDs were compared (Figure S4a & 2e). Precursor has two peaks at 235 and 288 nm corresponding to quinonoid and benzene rings, whereas N-CQDs have three peaks at 240, 295, and 395 nm originated from π - π∗ transition of CC, n - π∗ transition of CO, and aromatic π system [33–35]. Therefore, it was concluded that the N-CQDs were successfully carbonized. GSH possesses the peak at 206 nm and GSH-CQDs show strong absorption peaks around 210 nm, 240 nm, 295 nm, and 395 nm derived from GSH and N-CQDs. This indicates successful conjugation of GSH to the N-CQDs. Similar to optical properties of N-CQDs (Figure S4b), a bright green fluorescence of the GSH-CQDs under UV light with a maximum emission at 515 nm was observed upon excitation at 410 nm (Fig. 2e). The strong fluorescence of GSH-CQDs with a high quantum yield (QY) of 22.42 ± 6.88% were easily observed with the naked eye. The N-CQDs exhibit excitation-independent emission spectra (Figure S4c), however, the fluorescence emission spectra of GSH-CQDs exhibited an excitation-dependent feature (Fig. 2f). In general, the fluorescence mechanism of CQDs can be divided into two types: carbogenic core state emission (excitation-dependent) and surface molecular state emission (excitation-independent) [36,37]. Thus, it was assumed that the fluorescence of GSH-CQDs was achieved through surface molecular state emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of detection conditions for levodopa
ce:section.ce:para: To find the best experimental condition for levodopa detection, several parameters including pH, reaction temperature, GSH-CQDs concentration, and reaction time were evaluated. The effect of pH on fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0–F/F0, in which F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of the GSH-CQDs before and after the addition of levodopa) was depicted in Fig. 3 a. The fluorescence quenching efficiency increased dramatically as the pH increased from 5 to 11. As shown in Fig. 3b, the fluorescence quenching of the GSH-CQDs by levodopa reached the maximum value at 37 °C. To optimize the concentration of GSH-CQDs on levodopa detection, different concentrations (100–300 μg mL−1) of GSH-CQDs were incubated with levodopa. And, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the GSH-CQDs gradually increased and reached the peak at 250 μg mL−1 (Fig. 3c). Finally, as the reaction time between GSH-CQDs and levodopa increased, the fluorescence intensity of the GSH-CQDs progressively declined and did not decrease significantly after 30 min (Fig. 3d). From the obtained results, we determined the optimum conditions, such as pH 11, 37 °C, 250 μg mL−1 of the GSH-CQDs and reaction time of 30 min, for sensitive detection of levodopa.

The stability of GSH-CQDs is a prerequisite for fluorescence-based sensing applications. The photoluminescence (PL) intensities of GSH-CQDs against pH, temperature, oxidative stress and ionic strength were investigated (Figure S5). The fluorescence of GSH-CQDs remained stable over a pH range of 5–12 and within a temperature range of 27 °C–67 °C. Moreover, when various concentrations (100–500 μM) of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and sodium chloride (NaCl) were added to 250 μg mL−1 of GSH-CQDs, their fluorescence intensities were almost constant. That means high resistance of the GSH-CQDs to oxidative stress and ionic strength. In addition, long-term photostability of GSH-CQDs dissolved in aqueous solution was further investigated (Figure S6). The GSH-CQDs demonstrated stable appearance without a decrease in fluorescence intensity for more than two weeks. These results indicate that our GSH-CQDs have the stability against pH, temperature, oxidative stress, ionic strength, and long-term storage and can be utilized in our real life.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of levodopa
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the detection sensitivity of the GSH-CQDs fluorescent sensor, we performed the assay by adding various concentrations of levodopa (0–50 μM) to the GSH-CQDs and detecting the resulting fluorescence changes. Fig. 4 a showed that green fluorescence of the sensor GSH-CQDs was gradually quenched with increasing concentrations of levodopa, which was observed with the naked eye under UV-lamp irradiation at 365 nm. As the concentration of levodopa increased, the color of solution also become yellowish. For quantitative detection of levodopa in aqueous solution, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded with different concentrations of levodopa (Fig. 4b). The fluorescence emission intensity of GSH-CQDs at 515 nm decreased by ca. 62% at the levodopa concentration of 50 μM. These results indicate that the fluorescence quenching in GSH-CQDs depends on the concentration of levodopa. Also, no emission spectral shift of the GSH-CQDs was observed during the quenching process. Fig. 4c demonstrates that a linear correlation curve (y = 0.1799x+0.0133, R2 = 0.9981) was fitted between fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0–F/F0) of the GSH-CQDs and levodopa concentrations (0–1.0 μM). From the quantitative analysis, we determined the limit of detection (LOD) of our fluorescent sensor to be ∼0.057 μM.

To elucidate the detection mechanism of levodopa, we need to look into the interactions between GSH-CQDs and levodopa. As shown in Fig. 1, levodopa is oxidized to dopaquinone under alkaline conditions. This conversion was confirmed by solution color changes from colorless to yellow as well as the change in absorbance spectra (Figure S7). Since thiol groups (-SH) on the GSH-CQDs are rich in electrons and dopaquinone lacks electrons, nucleophilic thiol groups (-SH) of the GSH-CQDs can form covalent bonds with electrophilic dopaquinone through Michael-thiol addition [38–43]. Moreover, dopaquinone is known to act as an efficient fluorescence quencher for adjoining phosphors [44–46]. Therefore, this leads to photoinduced electron transfer between the GSH-CQDs and dopaquinone [47], resulting in fluorescence quenching of green fluorescent GSH-CQDs.

Additionally, to prove that the thiol group of GSH-CQDs binds to dopaquinone, 5,5′-dithiobis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) was used as a competitor to dopaquinone. It is reported that 5-thio-2-nitrobenzoate (TNB), the product between DTNB and thiols, can quench the fluorescence of fluorophore [48]. Because the absorbance spectra of TNB overlap with the excitation peak of GSH-CQDs (Figure S8a), the fluorescence of GSH-CQDs can be quenched by the inner filter effect (IFE) [49,50]. As shown in Figure S8b, GSH-CQDs emitted strong green fluorescence (Black line). When 500 μM of DTNB was added to GSH-CQDs, the fluorescence of GSH-CQDs was completely quenched (Red line). However, 50 μM levodopa-added GSH-CQDs showed ca. 62% fluorescence decrease (Blue line). After 50 μM of levodopa and 500 μM of DTNB sequentially reacted with GSH-CQDs, the fluorescence of GSH-CQDs was not completely quenched (ca. 80% decrease) (Green line). These results suggest that the thiol group of GSH-CQDs is a key binding site for recognition of dopaquinone. We further investigated the effect of the IFE or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) on the fluorescence quenching of GSH-CQDs by dopaquinone. As shown in Figure S9a, a slight spectra overlap was observed between the absorbance spectra of dopaquinone and fluorescence excitation spectra of GSH-CQDs, which indicates that the fluorescence quenching of GSH-CQDs may be associated with the IFE [49,50]. To further clarify the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the PL lifetimes of GSH-CQDs and GSH-CQDs + Dopaquinone were measured via time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC). Figure S9b displayed that the average PL lifetimes of GSH-CQDs and GSH-CQDs + Dopaquinone were 3.31 ns and 3.97 ns, respectively. The PL lifetimes were changed, thereby indicating that fluorescence quenching of GSH-CQDs is not due to IFE. And, the absorbance maximum of dopaquinone and the emission maximum of GSH-CQDs were located at 280 nm and 515 nm, respectively. Because their spectra rarely overlap, FRET cannot explain the quenching of the GSH-CQDs by dopaquinone. As a result, the photoinduced electron transfer from excited GSH-CQDs to dopaquinone is considered as a main mechanism of fluorescence quenching.

In order to validate that the fluorescence quenching in the GSH-CQDs fluorescent sensor is specifically caused by levodopa, we challenged our sensor with several unrelated biological substances (alanine, ascorbic acid, citric acid, cysteine, glutamine, glycine, and urea), quinone derivatives (anthraquinone, p-benzoquinone, and 1,4-naphthoquinone), and Parkinson’s medications (benserazide, carbidopa, and ropinirole). The concentration of non-target biological substances (250 μM), quinone derivatives (10 μM) and Parkinson’s medications (10 μM) used in this test was 250, 10, and 10 times higher than that of levodopa (1 μM), respectively. Compared to ca. 19% decrease of fluorescence intensity observed in 1 μM levodopa-added GSH-CQDs, other non-target substances produced the negligible fluorescence changes in the GSH-CQDs (Fig. 5 & Figure S10). To further demonstrate the specificity of GSH-CQDs for detection of levodopa, cysteine-conjugated carbon quantum dots (Cys-CQDs) were also prepared with the same synthesis method. Unlike optical properties of GSH-CQDs, Cys-CQDs exhibited a maximum emission at 510 nm upon excitation at 390 nm (Figure S11a). After reaction with 50 μM of levodopa, the fluorescence intensity of Cys-CQDs did not show any significant change (only 4% decrease) (Figure S11b). These results clearly demonstrate that the fluorescence quenching occurs through specific recognition between GSH-CQDs and levodopa.

Finally, to further investigate the clinical applicability of the developed GSH-CQDs fluorescent sensor, a spike-recovery test was performed. Levodopa spiked into human serum (2%) was incubated with the GSH-CQDs, and its corresponding concentration was calculated from the calibration curve (Fig. 4c). Table 1 a shows the spike and recovery results for the detection of levodopa in human serum. The recovery rate was in the range of 100.20–103.33%, with relative standard deviation (RSD) ranging from 0.98 to 3.98%. These results demonstrate that the proposed GSH-CQDs fluorescent sensor can detect levodopa in human serum with outstanding precision and reproducibility. Besides, analytical performance of our sensor was compared with that of previous works for levodopa detection. As presented in Table 1b, the detection sensitivity of our GSH-CQDs is superior to previously reported fluorescence methods or electrochemical methods. Consequently, the GSH-CQDs fluorescent sensor is useful for sensitive and selective detection of levodopa with practical applicability as an in vitro diagnostics sensor.

We have developed novel turn-off fluorescent optical sensors to detect levodopa. The prepared GSH-CQDs exhibit bright green fluorescence at 515 nm upon excitation at 410 nm and excellent stability against pH, temperature, oxidative stress, ionic strength, and long-term storage. Using the GSH-CQDs, we successfully detected levodopa under alkaline conditions and the resulting fluorescence quenching was detected by the naked eye under 365 nm UV light. The GSH-CQDs fluorescent sensor exhibited high specificity to levodopa over other non-target biomolecules, quinone derivatives, and Parkinson’s medications as well as superior sensitivity compared with previously reported methods. Furthermore, the GSH-CQDs successfully detected levodopa in human serum samples with excellent precision and recovery rates of 100.20–103.33%. The conversion of this concept study to practical applications requires improvement of the optimum conditions. If optimum operating conditions of the GSH-CQDs sensor are achieved as neutral pH, room temperature (25 °C), and reaction time less than 30 min, it would be more feasible for practical use of GSH-CQDs. Because this GSH-CQDs fluorescent sensor for the detection of levodopa is easy to synthesize, rapid, sensitive, and selective, this sensor is of great clinical value for the detection of levodopa to evaluate the condition of patients with Parkinson’s disease and prevent side effects.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Mid-Career Researcher Support Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT, and Future Planning (NRF-2018R1A2B6006815). This research was supported by the Technology Innovation Program funded by the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (20009356).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2021.338513.

Seok Won Park and Yun Kyung Jung designed the research, contributed to analysis, and wrote the manuscript and supporting text. Seok Won Park performed all experiments. Yun Kyung Jung provided financial support and supervised investigations. Tae Eun Kim contributed to analysis. All authors reviewed the manuscript.
